content:
Converting @Danilo Krummrich's platform driver sample to a documentation #Example / KUnit test [1] does work nicely.

However, in this test using assert_eq!() compilation fails with

error[E0425]: cannot find value `__DOCTEST_ANCHOR` in this scope
    --> rust/doctests_kernel_generated.rs:4252:102
     |
4252 |             kernel::kunit_assert_eq!("rust_doctest_kernel_property_rs_0", "rust/kernel/property.rs", __DOCTEST_ANCHOR - 150, $left, $right);
     |                                                                                                      ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ not found in this scope
...
4369 | assert_eq!(idx, Ok(0)); // FIXME: How to build this?
     | ---------------------- in this macro invocation
     |
     = note: this error originates in the macro `assert_eq` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

Any idea?

I worked around that with 'manual' if statements.

[1] 

/// # Examples
///
/// ```
/// # mod property_example {
/// #
/// # use kernel::{c_str, of, platform, prelude::*};
/// #
/// # struct PropertyExample {
/// #     pdev: platform::Device,
/// # }
/// #
/// # kernel::of_device_table!(
/// #     OF_TABLE,
/// #     MODULE_OF_TABLE,
/// #     <PropertyExample as platform::Driver>::IdInfo,
/// #     [(of::DeviceId::new(c_str!("test,rust-device")), ())]
/// # );
/// #
/// # impl platform::Driver for PropertyExample {
/// # type IdInfo = ();
/// # const ID_TABLE: platform::IdTable<Self::IdInfo> = &OF_TABLE;
/// #
/// # fn probe(
/// #   pdev: &mut platform::Device,
/// #   info: Option<&Self::IdInfo>
/// # ) -> Result<Pin<KBox<Self>>> {
/// # let dev = pdev.as_ref();
/// #
/// # dev_err!(dev, "The following two error messages are intended and correct from the tests:\n");
/// #
/// // Read an existing property as bool. This has two use cases:
/// // a) Get the value of a boolean property (true == exist, false == don't exist).
/// // b) Check if a property exists. Not limited to boolean properties.
/// let prop = dev.property_read::<bool>(c_str!("test,u32-prop"), None);
/// # //assert_eq!(prop, Ok(true)); // FIXME: How to build this?
/// # dev_info!(dev, "prop: {:?}\n", prop); // Debug only, drop later
/// if prop != Ok(true) {return Err(EINVAL);}
/// ...

The assert_eq!() macro uses __DOCTEST_ANCHOR which is auto-generated by scripts/rustdoc_test_gen.rs. But outside of the module mod property_example used here. So the assert_eq!() used inside the module doesn't "see" the __DOCTEST_ANCHOR placed outside the module by the generator. As a workaround I "manually" added that inside the module what makes it work then.

/// # mod property_example {
/// #
/// # // Get this into the scope of the module to make the assert_eq!() buildable
/// # static __DOCTEST_ANCHOR: i32 = core::line!() as i32 - 4;
/// ...

Not sure if there is something better :wink: I tried with super:: but without success.
Did you figure this out? The assert_eq macro itself is also written in the same scope as __DOCTEST_ANCHOR so I'm surprised that resolves without issue

Tamir Duberstein said:

Did you figure this out?

No, I used the workaround mentioned above "manually" adding __DOCTEST_ANCHOR.
Is the code available somewhere I can look at it?

The question we discussed back in November was if it is better to use a documentation #Example / kunit test or add something to samples/rust (and I think the answer was that its better to do documentation #Example / kunit tests, but in this special case samples/rust was preferred). So just for demonstration I tried to convert the samples/rust Danilo was in the process to upstream and which is used by the (still under development) Property/device tree patches into an #Example. Meanwhile samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs has been merged. So you could take that and convert it to an #Example like illustrated in the initial post [1] above. That should work nicely. And then try to add an assert_eq!(), for example checking Info() for 42. Or have a look to my RFC posting and search for assert_eq!() there :wink:
